coredata.dc:title: Construction of dual-channel ratio sensing platform and molecular logic gate for visual detection of oxytetracycline based on biomass carbon dots prepared from cherry tomatoes stalk
coredata.dc:creator: Arkin, Kamile
Zheng, Yuxin
Bei, Yuyang
Ma, Xuesong
Che, Weilong
Shang, Qingkun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, blue carbon dots (Stalk-Trp CDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield were successfully prepared from biological waste cherry tomato stalk and tryptophan. The morphology, composition, and properties of Stalk-Trp CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, infrared spectroscopy, ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. On this basis, a dual-channel ratio sensing platform (Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu) was constructed using the interaction of Stalk-Trp CDs with Eu3+, which reduced the detection limit of the antibiotic oxytetracycline from 0.15 µmol/L to 0.018 µmol/L. Channel 1 of the dual-channel ratio sensing platform is a neutral environment (pH = 6.5), and the specific response of the probe to OTC causes its fluorescence to change from blue to purple. Channel 2 is an alkaline environment (pH = 11.5), and the fluorescence of the probe turns green only in the presence of OTC. The interaction mechanism of the dual-channel ratio sensing platform with the antibiotics oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), and tetracycline (TC) was also revealed. The detection results of OTC in honey and milk by using Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu were highly consistent with the HPLC method recommended by the national standard. This shows that the dual-channel ratio sensing platform has great practical application value in the field of antibiotic detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass carbon dots
Ratio fluorescent probe
Dual-channel ratio sensing platform
Molecular logic gate
Antibiotic
Visual identification and detection

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Green and Sustainable Science and Technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs
Properties of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs
Effect of antibiotics on fluorescence intensity of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs
Construction of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu dual-channel ratio sensing platform
Response mechanism of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu to antibiotics
Application of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu to detect OTC in honey and milk

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs. (Inset: Particle size distribution).
The XPS(a-d), Raman(e) and FT-IR(f) spectra of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs.
UV–Vis spectra (a), excitation-emission matrix spectra of Stalk CDs (b), Trp CDs (c) and Stalk-Trp CDs (d).
The effect of antibiotics on the fluorescence of Stalk CDs or Stalk-Trp CDs.
The TEM/HRTEM (a), UV–Vis (b), and fluorescence spectra (c) of Stalk-Trp CDs @Eu.
The fluorescence spectra and linear relationship between Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu and CTC (a, d), TC (b, e) and OTC (c, f).
Fluorescence photos of solutions mixed with different concentrations of OTC and TC (CTC) under 365 nm UV lamp.
(a) The histogram of the normalized fluorescence intensity ratio of I617/I442, and (b) I505/I452. (c)The Logic gate circuit diagram and (d) the truth table of different input modes.
The fluorescence Excitation and Emission spectra of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu and UV–vis spectra of three antibiotics (a) and fluorescence decay curves (b, c, d).
Schematic Diagram of the Synthesis and Application Process.
Fluorescence lifetime of Eu3+ complexes and the number of water molecules.
Standard addition and detection results of OTC in honey and milk.
Comparisons of different methods to detect OTC.

head.ce:title: Construction of dual-channel ratio sensing platform and molecular logic gate for visual detection of oxytetracycline based on biomass carbon dots prepared from cherry tomatoes stalk

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, blue carbon dots (Stalk-Trp CDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield were successfully prepared from biological waste cherry tomato stalk and tryptophan. The morphology, composition, and properties of Stalk-Trp CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, infrared spectroscopy, ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. On this basis, a dual-channel ratio sensing platform (Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu) was constructed using the interaction of Stalk-Trp CDs with Eu3+, which reduced the detection limit of the antibiotic oxytetracycline from 0.15 µmol/L to 0.018 µmol/L. Channel 1 of the dual-channel ratio sensing platform is a neutral environment (pH = 6.5), and the specific response of the probe to OTC causes its fluorescence to change from blue to purple. Channel 2 is an alkaline environment (pH = 11.5), and the fluorescence of the probe turns green only in the presence of OTC. The interaction mechanism of the dual-channel ratio sensing platform with the antibiotics oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), and tetracycline (TC) was also revealed. The detection results of OTC in honey and milk by using Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu were highly consistent with the HPLC method recommended by the national standard. This shows that the dual-channel ratio sensing platform has great practical application value in the field of antibiotic detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new member of the carbon nanomaterial family, carbon dots (CDs) are usually spherical fluorescent nanoparticles with a size of 0 ∼ 10 nm. The CDs mainly take sp2 hybrid conjugated carbon as the core, together with numerous oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups in the periphery, to form a “core-shell” structure [1]. CDs are usually characterized by low preparation cost, good light stability, easy surface modification, and low biological toxicity, and have been widely used in biosensors [2], light-emitting diodes [3], photocatalysis [4,5], analysis and detection [6] and other fields.

In recent years, it has been found that many waste biomass materials in nature can be used as cheap carbon sources for preparing CDs [7]. These organics can produce CDs with more oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on the surface during the carbonization process. The reported biomass carbon sources for CDs preparation include cherry tomato [8], rice husk [9], pear peel [10], garlic [11], oatmeal [12], silkworm chrysalis [13], and Taxus leaves [14]. For example, Du et al [8] prepared the blue carbon quantum dots with a fluorescence quantum yield of 9.7% using fresh cherry tomatoes (edible part) and designed a fluorescence probe to detect trifluralin in soil.

Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is one of the key factors that determine the application field and effectiveness of carbon dots. The QY of many CDs directly prepared from biomass is low. Many scholars have proved that the QY can be effectively improved by nitrogen doping or surface modification of CDs with nitrogen-containing compounds [15]. Luo et al. [16] used a microwave-assisted method to synthesize blue fluorescent CDs with pear juice as carbon source and ethylenediamine as nitrogen source. The introduction of ethylenediamine increased QY to 32.6%. Shang et al. [17] prepared CDs from biomass broccoli stems by one-step hydrothermal method and modified them with polyethylene imine (PEI), which increased the QY from 13.24% to 18.34%.

As fluorescent probes, CDs have played an increasingly important role in the field of analysis and detection. The detection of antibiotic residues in environmental samples is the premise to further eliminate antibiotic pollution. Taking tetracyclines (TCs) as an example, detection methods include ultra-high performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS/MS) [18], colorimetry [19], electrochemical analysis [20] and fluorescence analysis [21–24]. Among them, the fluorescence method has the advantages of simple operation, fast and high sensitivity, but it needs to use organic reagents for derivatization, which may cause secondary pollution to the environment. Some scholars have realized the detection of tetracycline antibiotics by CDs fluorescence probe. For example, Ma et al. [23] synthesized blue-green fluorescent CDs through one-step heat treatment of phthalic acid and ethylenediamine to visually detect tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC) and chlortetracycline (CTC). The detection limits were 50 nmol/L, 36 nmol/L, and 373 nmol/L, respectively. Because the structure of TCs is very similar, researchers are still facing serious challenges to achieve visual identification and selective detection of these antibiotics using CDs fluorescent probes, especially biomass CDs. Like many rare earth complexes, europium complexes have high photoluminescence efficiency, narrow emission band, and high color purity. TCs can use their β - diketone structure with Eu3+ to form stable complexes (Eu-TCs), which produce red light emission with wavelength 617 nm. It is greatly different from the fluorescence emission of CDs (blue or green emission), which is very conducive to the construction of a ratio fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of TCs [25]. In addition, the ultraviolet radiation of the excited CDs can be effectively transferred to Eu3+ through the “antenna effect”, which enhances the fluorescence emission characteristics of Eu3+complexes[21,24]. The probe derived from CDs and Eu3+is expected to achieve a wide range of fluorescence changes from blue to red and high sensitivity ratio fluorescence detection of TCs.

In the electronic computer, the logic gate is the basis for the realization of logic operations, usually composed of transistors. After two currents with different levels pass through this “gate”, they will generate “high” and “low” signals, representing “true” and “false” on the logic gate, or “1″ or ”0″ in the binary. Molecular switches and logic gates have obvious similarities. The molecular switch converts the input stimulus signals (such as chemical signals, electrical signals, optical signals, etc.) into output signals, so the binary logic rules can also be applied to the signals of the molecular switch, thus constructing one of the main components of the molecular computer-molecular logic gate [26]. Among all kinds of molecular switches, fluorescent switches have attracted much attention because of their high sensitivity, simple operation, and easy detection of signal output. In recent years, a large number of molecular logic gates based on fluorescence switch properties have been reported [27,28]. Multi-input molecular logic gates based on carbon dots with multi-color fluorescence emission will have great application potential in the fields of information processing, medical diagnosis, ion detection, and drug recognition [29].

In this work, we successfully prepared blue CDs (Stalk-Trp CDs) with high QY using a discarded stalk of cherry tomato (inedible part) as carbon source and tryptophan as nitrogen source (as shown in Scheme 1 ). The reason why tryptophan is selected as nitrogen source is its biological characteristics, rich in amino/carboxyl groups, and its conjugated structure, which may affect the fluorescence characteristics of CDs. Although the content of the three antibiotics CTC, TC, and OTC can be determined by fluorescence quenching method, it is difficult to distinguish and identify them. Next, we designed and constructed the dual-channel ratio sensing platform Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu. The relative intensity difference between the purple fluorescence emitted by Eu3+-antibiotic complexes at 617 nm and the blue fluorescence emitted by Stalk-Trp CDs at 442 nm greatly improved the sensitivity of antibiotic detection. Through the design of molecular logic gates, the visual recognition of OTC is realized. Finally, we also tested the effect of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu on the detection of OTC in real samples of honey and milk, and the results were in high agreement with the method recommended by the national standard. This work will provide new ideas for the in-depth development and utilization of waste biomass to prepare high value-added products, and provide a new method for the visual detection of antibiotics.

Europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu (NO3)3·6H2O) was obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chlortetracycline (CTC), Tetracycline (TC), Ciprofloxacin (CIP), Levofloxacin (LEV), Fleroxacin (FLE), Pefloxacin (PEF), Lomefloxacin (LOM), Oxytetracycline (OTC), Cysteine (Cys), Histidine (His), Valine (Val), Threonine (Thr), Phenylalanine (Phe), Leucine (Leu), Tryptophan (Trp) and Trichloroacetic acid were purchased from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cherry tomatoes, milk, and honey samples were purchased from local supermarkets. The water used in the experiment was ultrapure water obtained by Millipore. All chemical reagents are of analytical grade without further purification.

The morphology of CDs is characterized by field emission transmission electron microscopy (FEI Tecnai G2 F20). The absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of CDs are determined using an ultraviolet spectrophotometer (Thermo Genesys 10S) and an F-320 fluorescence spectrophotometer (GANGDONG SCI.@TECH. CO.LTD). The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) was measured using Nicolet 6700 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested on Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The Raman spectra of CDs were measured by Raman spectrometer (Labram Hr Evolution). The determination of antibiotics recommended by GB/T 18932.4–2002 and GB/T 21317–2007 is completed by using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Agilent 1200). The fluorescence lifetime of CDs was measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000).

CDs prepared from the stalk of cherry tomatoes by hydrothermal method are labeled as Stalk CDs. 0.5 g of lyophilized powder of cherry tomatoes stalk was dispersed in 10 mL of ultrapure water. It was moved to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave after sonication for 10 min and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 14 h. After the samples were naturally cooled to room temperature, they were centrifuged and filtered to obtain a dark brown solution (named Stalk CDs), which was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.
If tryptophan (0.15 g) was used instead of the stalk of cherry tomatoes, the solution prepared under the same conditions was named Trp CDs.

If 0.5 g of freeze-dried powder of cherry tomatoes stalk and 0.15 g of tryptophan were dispersed into 10 mL of ultrapure water together, the CDs obtained by the same method were named as Stalk-Trp CDs.

2 mL of water and 5 mL of 40 mmol/L Eu (NO3)3·6H2O were added to 3 mL of Stalk-Trp CDs solution. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 2 h and then centrifuged (10,000 rpm). The precipitate was washed with ultrapure water and dried to obtain Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu.

The relative fluorescence QY of CDs was calculated according to the equation using quinine sulfate as a reference solution:
Φ x
=
Φ R
n x
n R
A R
A x
η
x
2
η
R
2
where Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, η is the refractive index of the solvent (ηH2O = 1.336), A represents the absorbance, n is the integrated area of the emission peak, and the subscripts R and × represent the reference solution, and the CDs, respectively. The concentration of quinine sulfate solution is 0.1 mol/L (Φ R = 54%).

A certain concentration and volume of amino acids (Cys, His, Val, Thr, Phe, Trp) or antibiotics (CTC, TC, CIP, LEV, FLE, PEF, LOM) or metal salt solutions (Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, K+, Na+) was added to the CDs solution, stirred for 3 min to make the mixture uniform, and then measured its emission spectrum under 390 nm. The effects of amino acids, antibiotics, or metal salts on the fluorescence intensity of CDs were investigated. The experiment was repeated 3 times.

Referring to literature [24] with minor modifications: An appropriate amount of trichloroacetic acid (300 mg/mL) was added to milk (volume ratio is 5:100). The mixed liquid was sonicated for 20 min, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to remove lipids and proteins. The supernatant obtained after filtration was diluted 100 times for use.
The honey sample was directly diluted 100 times with deionized water and filtered with a 0.22 µm membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM and HRTEM images of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs are shown in Fig. 1 . It can be seen that both of them are quasi-spherical, with an average diameter of 2.25 nm and 13.52 nm, respectively, and a clear lattice structure can be observed by HRTEM. The lattice fringe spacings are 0.333 nm and 0.345 nm, respectively, corresponding to the crystal planes of graphite (002).

As shown in Fig. 2 a, the elemental peaks of C, N, and O are visible in the XPS full spectra of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs. Three peaks at 284.6, 286.2, and 288.4 eV were observed in the high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2b), which were attributed to the C = O, C-O/C-N, and C = C bonds in Stalk CDs. For Stalk-Trp CDs all peaks are shifted towards the higher binding energy. The two peaks at 398.6 and 399.6 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of N1s of Stalk CDs (Fig. 2c) are attributed to C = N and N-H bonds, respectively. There are only N-H bonds and C-N-C bonds in the N1s spectrum of Stalk-Trp CDs, and the N-H bonding energy is shifted to the lower binding energy direction. The O1s spectra of both Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs (Fig. 2d) can be resolved into two peaks at 531.8 and 532.8 eV, corresponding to C = O and C-O bonds, but the two peaks of Stalk-Trp CDs are oriented towards high binding energies offset, and the relative intensities of the two peaks are reversed. The above changes indicate that the chemical bond environment is slightly different in Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs. The nitrogen contents of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs were also calculated to be 3.17% and 5.68% (N/C ratios were 0.042 and 0.080, respectively) based on XPS peak areas. This is the result of the introduction of tryptophan.

In the Raman spectra of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs (Fig. 2e), there are two peaks at 1388 cm−1 (D band) and 1590 cm−1 (G band), corresponding to the disordered structures in planar graphite sp3 and sp2 clusters crystals, respectively. The ID/IG represents the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon atoms. The calculated ID/IG ratios of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs are 0.83 and 0.86, respectively, indicating that both CDs have a highly graphitized and graphitic-like structure. The FT-IR spectra of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs are shown in Fig. 2f. From the position and intensity of the absorption peaks, the two CDs have roughly the same functional groups, such as O-H/N-H, C = O/C = C, C-O, C-N, etc.

The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs are shown in Fig. 3 . For comparison, the absorption and fluorescence spectra of Trp CDs (Trp as carbon source) and the linear superposition of the absorption spectra of Stalk CDs and Trp CDs are also shown. From Fig. 3a it can be seen that Stalk CDs have a strong absorption at 200 nm and a weak absorption at 275 nm. Trp CDs have obvious absorption peaks at 220 nm and 280 nm, respectively, and the maximum absorption peak is located at 220 nm. Two new shoulders appeared at 250 nm and 304 nm for Stalk-Trp CDs, in addition to the strong absorption at 200 nm. It is quite different from the linear superposition of Stalk CDs and Trp CDs. This suggests that there must be an interaction between Stalk and Trp during the hydrothermal process. The introduction of tryptophan into the reaction system increased the conjugated structure of the generated Stalk-Trp CDs, resulting in more pronounced π-π transition and n-π transition characteristics.

The excitation-emission matrix Stalk CDs, Trp CDs, and Stalk-Trp CDs are shown in Fig. 3(b-d). The optimal excitation wavelength for Stalk CDs is 370 nm and the optimal emission wavelength is 440 nm. For Stalk-Trp CDs, those wavelength are at 390 nm and 450 nm, respectively. The fluorescence characteristics of Trp CDs are quite different from the above two CDs, the optimal excitation wavelength is 350 nm, and the optimal emission wavelength is 442 nm. It can be seen that Stalk-Trp CDs retain more features of Stalk CDs. Under 365 nm UV light irradiation, all three CDs exhibited blue fluorescence. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the QY of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs were determined and calculated to be 7.95% and 30.77%, respectively. Under the same conditions, the fluorescence quantum yield of Trp CDs, which is 12.98%, is higher than that of Stalk CDs. Thus, the introduction of tryptophan with a certain conjugated structure greatly improves the fluorescence QY of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence stability of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs under different time, continuous illumination, different pH, and ionic strength were investigated, as shown in Figure S1. The two CDs solutions were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C, and the fluorescence spectra were measured every 5 days. It was found that the fluorescence intensity remained above 95% after 30 days (Fig. S1a). From Fig. S1b, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensities of the two CDs are almost constant even at high concentrations of NaCl (1000 µmol/L), which is beneficial for their applications in various physiological environments. In the case of continuous illumination, the fluorescence spectra were measured every 1 min. We found that the fluorescence intensity of Stalk CD and Stalk-Trp CD decreased by 12% and 22%, respectively, after 60 min (shown in Fig. S1c). This may be fluorescence quenching due to aggregation. It can be seen from Fig. S1d that with the gradual increase of pH, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually weakened until quenched. It was found from the absorption spectra of the two CDs at different pH conditions (shown in Fig. S1e and f) that with the increase of pH value of the solution, their absorbance at the optimum excitation wavelength (370 nm and 390 nm) of the CDs gradually decreased, and the absorbance was almost zero at pH = 14, which was the main reason for the fluorescence quenching of the CDs under strongly alkaline conditions. The XPS and FT-IR spectra have confirmed that the surfaces of the two CDs contain amino, hydroxy, or carboxyl groups, which are protonated in strong acidic solution and gradually deprotonated with the increase of pH, leading to fluorescence quenching of the carbon dots. For Stalk CDs, the fluorescence intensity was almost unchanged in the range of pH = 4 to 10, but it decreased sharply when the pH was greater than 10. The dependence of Stalk-Trp CDs on pH was different from that of Stalk CDs. When pH increased from 6 to 8, the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply. This may be attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of Stalk-Trp CDs containing more acidic or basic groups with the increase of pH of the solution. From Fig. 1c and d, it can be seen that the intensity of –NH and C = O peaks on the surface of Stalk-Trp CDs increases, indicating that the introduction of Trp increases the number of carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of antibiotics on fluorescence intensity of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Nine antibiotics were mixed with Stalk CDs or Stalk-Trp CDs, respectively, and the fluorescence spectra of CDs were measured after stirring for 3 min, as shown in Fig. 4 . It can be seen from Fig. 4a that antibiotics CTC, OTC, and TC can quench the fluorescence of Stalk CDs to the same extent, while the other six antibiotics do not affect the fluorescence of Stalk CDs. It is found from Fig. 4b that antibiotics CTC, OTC, and TC have different fluorescence quenching on Stalk-Trp CDs, among which CTC has the greatest quenching effect, and the other six antibiotics have no effect on the fluorescence of Stalk-Trp CDs. OTC, CTC, and TC belong to tetracycline antibiotics (TCs). They have similar polycyclic structures and light absorption characteristics at 300–400 nm. The optimal excitation wavelengths of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs are 370 nm and 390 nm respectively. The absorption spectra of OTC, CTC, and TC cover the excitation wavelengths of the Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs (370 nm and 390 nm), and the fluorescence of the two CDs is quenched due to the internal filtering effect [30].

The effects of the concentrations of CTC, OTC, and TC on the fluorescence intensities of the two CDs were further investigated. The results are exhibited in Figure S2 and S3. The fluorescence intensity of Stalk CDs decreased as the concentration of three antibiotics increased. According to 3σ/k [31] and the slope of the linear equation, the detection limits of Stalk CDs for CTC, TC, and OTC can be calculated as 0.55, 0.43, and 0.43 μmol/L, respectively. Since the structures of CTC, TC, and OTC are very similar, their effect on the fluorescence intensity of Stalk CDs is the same, so the detection limit is almost the same. As can be seen from Fig. 4b, the same concentrations of CTC, OTC, and TC have slightly different effects on the fluorescence intensity of Stalk-Trp CDs. The data in Figure S3 also show that the concentration curves of the three antibiotics versus the fluorescence intensity of Stalk-Trp CDs are also slightly different. The obtained Stalk-Trp CDs have detection limits of 0.19, 0.17, and 0.15 μmol/L for CTC, TC, and OTC.

When the same concentration of metal ions, amino acids, and antibiotics coexisted with one of CTC, TC, and OTC, the effect on the fluorescence intensity of the two CDs were investigated. The results (listed in Figure S4) indicated that these coexisting substances did not further alter the fluorescence intensity of Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs, which means they did not interfere with the detection of the three antibiotics by these CDs.

The above results show that the three antibiotics can be quantified separately according to the linear relationship between the concentration of CTC, TC, and OTC and the fluorescence quenching of Stalk CDs or Stalk-Trp CDs, but we cannot identify which antibiotic in the mixed system, because these three antibiotics merely attenuate or quench the blue fluorescence of CDs and do not change the color of the emitted light. It is extremely necessary to construct a fluorescent probe that can judge the presence of three antibiotics by the change of fluorescent color.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu dual-channel ratio sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Europium nitrate is added in the hydrothermal process to obtain Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu. From the XPS spectra of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu (shown in Fig. S5 a), the Eu 3d peak is visible in addition to the C1s, N1s and O1s peaks. The four peaks with binding energies of 1134.1 eV, 1164.3 eV, 1124.12 eV and 1154.2 eV in Fig. S4b belongs to the Eu3+ 3d5/2, Eu3+ 3d3/2, Eu2+ 3d5/2 and Eu2+ 3d3/2 energy levels, respectively, which indicates that the Stalk-Trp CDs and Eu3+ have been successfully combined. The TEM/HRTEM, UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectra of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu are presented in Fig. 5. The TEM and HRTEM images of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu are shown in Fig. 5a. Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu is quasi-spherical with an average diameter of 14.09 nm and a clear lattice structure can be observed by HRTEM. The lattice fringe spacings is 0.328 nm, corresponding to the crystal planes of graphite (002). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of Stalk-Trp CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu is shown in Fig. 5b. Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu has two new absorption peaks that appear at 220 nm and 318 nm. The former is derived from Eu3+, and the latter is the result of the red shift of the absorption peak of 304 nm derived from Stalk-Trp CDs due to the interaction between Eu3+and Stalk-Trp CDs. The absorption peak (205 nm) generated by π → π* transition still exists, indicating the similarity of the main structure of the two CDs. The fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu are shown in Fig. 5c, which is a single emission peak corresponding to an emission wavelength of 450 nm when the excitation wavelength is 390 nm.

When the nine antibiotics were mixed with Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu, only CTC, TC, and OTC reduced the blue fluorescence of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu (Figure S6). But the OTC-containing system showed a new emission peak at 617 nm, making the solution appear purple under a 365 nm UV lamp. This facilitates rapid identification of OTC and further construction of a dual-channel ratio sensing platform to improve detection sensitivity. Coexisting substances of the same concentration, such as metal ions, amino acids, and other antibiotics, do not affect the fluorescence emission intensity ratio I617/I442 (see Figure S6).

The effect of the concentration of antibiotic CTC, TC, and OTC on the fluorescence emission intensity of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu is investigated. The pH of the solution is controlled to 6.5 by the buffer solution. As shown in Fig. 6 a ∼ c, the fluorescence emission intensity of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu at 442 nm gradually decreased and became a linear quenching as the concentration of CTC, TC, and OTC increased. Moreover, a new emission peak at 617 nm appeared and gradually enhanced, especially for OTC. The purple color of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu + OTC is the result of red plus blue. Fig. 6d ∼ f shows the linear relationship between the antibiotic concentration of antibiotic and I617/I442. Here y represents I617/I442, and × is the concentration of CTC, TC, and OTC, and their correlation coefficients are listed in Table S3. In different linear ranges, the concentration of antibiotics has a good linear relationship with I617/I442. According to 3σ/ slope, the detection limit of CTC, TC, and OTC is 0.23, 0.16, and 0.018 μmol/L by Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu. Compared with Stalk CDs and Stalk-Trp CDs, the detection sensitivity of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu for OTC was increased more than eight times. Because the detection of OTC is determined by the fluorescence quenching at 442 nm and the fluorescence enhancement at 617 nm together, thus more interference from other factors is eliminated. The UV–Vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of Eu3+ in the presence of different concentrations of OTC, CTC and TC are shown in Figure S7. In the presence of antibiotics, there are 273 nm and 381 nm absorption peaks in the absorption spectra, and the peak intensity increases with the increase of antibiotic concentration. This is caused by the electronic transition of the complex formed by antibiotic and Eu3+. Eu-TCs complex has several weak fluorescence emission peaks, among which the emission peaks at 450 nm and 617 nm are more obvious after amplification. The general trend is that with the increase of antibiotic concentration, the fluorescence emission at 450 nm decreases and the fluorescence emission at 617 nm increases. Taking I450/I617 as the ordinate and antibiotic concentration as the abscissa, three standard curves are obtained. The detection limits of Eu-TCs complex on CTC, TC, and OTC are calculated to be 1.87, 8.47, and 1.82 μmol/L respectively. Compared with the detection results by Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu, the sensitivity is reduced by 8, 53, and 101 times respectively.

How to use the special response of OTC to Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu to distinguish whether there is OTC in the system? We added the probe Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu to the CTC-OTC or TC-OTC mixed solution. Fig. 7 shows the specific identification of OTC in mixed solution by a dual-channel ratio sensing platform. When the pH of the detection environment is 6.5, the detection process is Channel 1. With the increase of OTC concentration, the blue fluorescence of the probe gradually weakens and changes to blue-violet, purple, and purple-red fluorescence. When CTC or TC with 10 μmol/L or 50 μmol/L coexists with OTC, the fluorescence color of the probe is almost the same as that of OTC only, except that the blue fluorescence quenching is more obvious. When 100 μmol/L CTC or TC coexists with OTC, the blue fluorescence quenching is extremely serious, only when the OTC concentration is 40 μmol/L it will output dark purple. The detection environment of Channel 2 is alkaline (pH = 11.5). With the increase of OTC concentration, the output gradually changes from blue to green. When 10 μmol/L or 50 μmol/L CTC or TC coexists with OTC, the output result is almost the same as when OTC exists alone, and the blue fluorescence quenching is more obvious when the concentration of CTC or TC is 50 μmol/L. When 100 μmol/L CTC or TC coexists with OTC, the blue fluorescence quenching is very serious until the OTC concentration reaches 40 μmol/L, then Channel 2 could output dark green.

The influence of the three antibiotics on the fluorescence spectrum of the probe at pH = 11.5 is shown in Figure S8. OTC makes the emission peak of the probe red shift from 452 nm to 505 nm, while CTC and TC only make the fluorescence of the probe quenched and the emission peak broadened, but the red shift is not obvious. This is caused by the aggregation-induced fluorescence phenomenon produced by the antibiotics themselves in the three systems (see Figure S9 and S10 of the supporting information for details). When the concentration ratio of CTC or TC to OTC is 3:1, bright green fluorescence can be observed in the ultraviolet lamp at 365 nm. However, the mixture of CTC and TC in any proportion can only quench the blue fluorescence of the probe. Therefore, the dual channel ratio sensing platform can realize the visual recognition of OTC specificity in the mixed solution of tetracycline antibiotics.

Based on the dual-channel ratio sensing platform of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu to OTC, we designed two “INHIBIT” molecular logic gates that combine visual sensing with intelligent molecular computing (Fig. 8 ). In Channel 1, I617/I442 = 0.4 is used as the logic gate switch valve, because the purple color produced by the interaction between OTC (30 μmol/L) and Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu (0.45 mg/mL) is visible to the naked eye under UV light. The logic gate uses OTC and TC (CTC) as two inputs, while the ratiometric fluorescence of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu (I617/I442) is used as the output. According to Fig. 8a, if the I617/I442 is greater than 0.4, the output is “ON”; if it is below 0.4, the output is “OFF”. In Channel 2, I505/I452 = 1.0 is used as the logic gate switch valve (shown in Fig. 8b) due to the green color being clear to naked eye under UV light when the concentration of OTC is 10 μmol/L. In contrast, the concentration of OTC meeting the threshold in Channel 2 is lower than that in Channel 1, so it is more sensitive.

Fig. 8d summarizes these corresponding symbols and the resulting truth table for the different input modes. Under Channel 1 (pH = 6.5) in the absence of OTC, I617/I442 cannot reach the threshold value of the logic gate, thus the blue fluorescence is still present and the output is (1,0). When the OTC concentration is greater than 30 µmol/L, the fluorescent emission channel at 617 nm is opened and purple fluorescence appeared, thus the output is (0,1). Similarly, under Channel 2 (pH = 11.5) in the absence of OTC, I505/I452 cannot reach the threshold value of the logic gate, so the blue fluorescence is still present and the output is (1,0). When the OTC concentration is greater than 10 µmol/L, the fluorescent emission channel at 505 nm is opened and green fluorescence appeared, thus the output is (0,1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu to antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para:
Generally, the fluorescence quenching of CDs probe by antibiotics may originate from two mechanisms: Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE). According to the FRET theory, the spectral overlap of the donor and the acceptor is the premise of resonance energy transfer, and the distance between them is the key to the occurrence of resonance energy transfer. The distance (r) between the donor Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu and acceptor antibiotic can be calculated according to the following equations [32–35]:
(1)
E = 1 -
I /
I 0
(2)
E
50 %
=
1
1 +
r
R 0
6
(3)
R
0
6
= 8.8 ×
10
- 15
κ
2
n
- 4
ϕ D
J
λ
(4)
A λ
=
ε λ
b c
(5)
J
λ
=
∫
0
∞
F D
λ
ε A
λ
λ
4
d λ
∫
0
∞
F D
λ
d λ
where E is the energy transfer efficiency between the Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu and OTC, I and I0 are the fluorescence intensities of donor Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu with and without acceptor antibiotic, respectively. E50% is 50% of E. R0 is Förster distance. FD(λ) is the emission spectral area of the donor, λ is the excitation wavelength of the Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu, εA(λ) is the molar absorbance index of the acceptor at the excitation wavelength λ, κ2 is the orientation factor (the value of κ2 is 2/3 due to the dynamic averaging of the donor–acceptor system). ΦD is the quantum yield of the donor and n is the refractive index of the medium (the refractive index of H2O is 1.33). The calculated distances r between Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu and CTC, TC, and OTC are 16.09 nm, 13.31 nm, and 14.39 nm, respectively. Since the distances r are relatively long (greater than 10 nm), the possibility of energy resonance transfer between them is less.

It can be seen from Fig. 9 a that the absorption spectra of the three antibiotics partially overlap with the excitation spectrum of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu. However, there is little overlap with the emission spectrum of the Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu. The effects of three antibiotics on the fluorescence lifetime of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu are shown in Fig. 9b-d. It can be seen that antibiotics hardly affect the fluorescence decay curve of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu. Fitting the first-order function to the fluorescence decay curve, the obtained fluorescence lifetime of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu was 14.47 ns, which were 14.10, 14.33, and 14.32 ns after adding OTC, CTC, and TC, respectively. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu (at 442 nm) by antibiotics is mainly attributed to IFE.

To reveal why only OTC can interact with the probe to make Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu change from blue to purple fluorescence, the fluorescence spectra of the complexes formed by three kinds of antibiotics with Eu3+ were investigated and shown in Figure S11. It is obvious that all three complexes can generate fluorescence emission at 617 nm under 390 nm excitation, but the emission intensity of Eu3+-OTC complex is the strongest, while that of Eu3+-CTC complex is the weakest.

Normally, the coordination number of Eu3+ is 6. From the above analysis, we can see that under our experimental conditions, there is no strong interaction between Eu3+and Stalk-Trp CDs. It is possible that the Stalk-Trp CDs and water molecules coordinate with Eu3+ at the same time, and the proportion of water molecules is large. According to Formula 6 [36], the numbers of water molecules coordinated with Eu3+ in the presence of probe or antibiotic are calculated by investigating the fluorescence lifetime of the Eu complex in H2O and D2O, which are listed in Table 1 .
(6)
q = 1.1
1
τ
H 2
O
-
1
τ
D 2
O
- 0.31
where q is the number of water molecules and τH2O and τD2O are the fluorescence lifetime of Eu3+complexes in H2O and D2O, respectively. It can be seen from Table 1 that for Eu-CTC, Eu-TC, and Eu-OTC complexes, the number of water molecules involved in coordination is 5, 5, and 4. This shows that only one CTC or TC molecule is coordinated with Eu, while two OTC molecules are coordinated with Eu3+ (or CTC and TC coordinate with Eu3+ with monodentate ligand, while OTC coordinate with Eu with bidentate ligand). It is generally believed that the more the number of antibiotic molecules, the larger the conjugate plane and the stronger the rigidity of the formed complex, and ultimately the stronger the fluorescence emission. This may be the main reason for the different fluorescence emission intensities of the three complexes (as shown in Figure S11).

The q value of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu is 4, which indicates that the coordination number of Eu3+ and Stalk-Trp CDs is 2. It may be the result of the chelation of Eu3+ with two hydroxyl groups or one carboxyl group in Stalk. When the probe Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu interacts with OTC, CTC, and TC respectively, the number of water molecules coordinated with Eu3+ decreases to 2, 3, and 3, indicating that there are still water molecules coordinated with Eu3+ in addition to antibiotic molecules. When water molecules absorb ultraviolet light, they will generate high-frequency vibration, which will lead to the loss of fluorescence emission energy of the complexes formed by Eu and antibiotics. The more water molecules, the greater the energy loss. This may be the main reason why purple fluorescence can be observed in Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu + OTC system, but not in Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu + CTC and Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu + TC system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu to detect OTC in honey and milk
ce:section.ce:para: When the Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu probe is added to the pretreated honey and milk, blue fluorescence can be observed under 365 nm ultraviolet light. The fluorescence intensity was not weakened compared with pure Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu probe and purple fluorescence did not appear. This is consistent with (1,0) in the logic gate shown in Fig. 8, that is, honey and milk samples do not contain CTC, TC, or OTC (or the content is lower than the detection limits of Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu probe). After OTC standard solution is added to milk and honey, bright purple fluorescence can be observed, which conforms to (0,1) in the logic gate. The measured I617/I442 is taken into the equation y = 0.110 + 0.013x (listed in Table S3), and the concentration of OTC is calculated. The concentration of OTC added and the measured results are listed in Table 2 . When the spiking concentration in honey sample is 10 µmol/L and 20 µmol/L, the measured values are 9.68 and 21.39 µmol/L, and the standard deviations of three parallel determinations are 2.19% and 1.69%. Similarly, the determination results in milk samples were 10.53 µmol/L and 19.60 µmol/L, with relative standard deviations of 2.12% and 3.39%. The same sample is determined by HPLC recommended by national standards (GB/T 18932.4-2002, GB/T 21317–2007). The maximum deviation of the two methods is 1.57 µmol/L, and the minimum deviation is 0.3 µmol/L. Furthermore, we calculated the Pearson correlation coefficient of the detection results using the Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu probe and HPLC methods. The coefficient is 0.9929, indicating that the test results of the two methods are highly consistent. This means the Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu probe can be used to detect OTC accurately.

In recent years, many methods for rapid detection of OTC with different sensing materials have been reported, such as the electrochemical method, colorimetric method, and fluorescence method (see Table 3 ). Although the detection limit of electrochemical method is low (0.007 nmol/L), the preparation of the electrode and its reproducibility needs to be improved. CDs prepared by different methods have different linear ranges and detection limits for OTC detection. Compared with the detection results by similar materials such as SiCDs@mMIPs-cit-Eu, N-CDs, and Cysteamine-AuNPs, the dual-channel ratio sensing platform we designed and constructed from waste biomass has a wider linear range and lower detection limit for OTC detection. More importantly, it can accurately identify the presence of OTC in TCs mixed solution. In addition, it can realize the rapid and parallel detection of three tetracycline antibiotics (CTC, OTC, TC) in one detection cycle. When the OTC concentration exceeds the preset threshold, the platform can automatically convert the fluorescence signal. Based on this dual-channel ratio sensing platform and molecular logic gate, color and data can be collected with smartphones, and semi-quantitative automatic detection can be carried out. It can also be made into test strips for on-site inspection [37].

The addition of tryptophan as a nitrogen source during the preparation of CDs from discarded cherry tomato stalk increased the QY from 7.95% (Stalk CDs) to 30.77% (Stalk-Trp CDs). Except for three antibiotics, CTC, TC, and OTC, which could quench the fluorescence of two CDs, other antibiotics or metal ions and amino acids did not affect on the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs. The detection limit of CTC, TC, and OTC was reduced from 0.55, 0.43, and 0.43 μmol/L (by Stalk CDs) to 0.19, 0.17, and 0.15 μmol/L (by Stalk-Trp CDs) via fluorescence quenching of CDs. Using the interaction between Stalk-Trp CDs and Eu3+, the dual-channel ratio sensing platform Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu was constructed. Due to the influence of OTC on the fluorescence intensity ratio I617/I442 of the probe, the detection limit of OTC was further reduced to 0.018 μmol/L. On this basis, a dual-channel ratio sensing platform was designed. Channel 1 is a neutral environment (pH = 6.5), and the specific response of the probe to OTC causes its fluorescence to change from blue to purple. Channel 2 is an alkaline environment (pH = 11.5), and the fluorescence of the probe turns green only in the presence of OTC. Molecular logic gates are designed to realize the visual recognition of the presence of OTC. Further research found that the quenching of the blue fluorescence of the Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu probe by antibiotics is due to IFE rather than FRET, and the generation of purple fluorescence is the result of the coordination between Eu3+ and the antibiotic. The different responses of OTC, CTC, and TC to the Stalk-Trp CDs@Eu probe are caused by the resonance effect of the water molecules bound to them. OTC in honey and milk were determined by a probe and standard method (HPLC), respectively, and the results were highly consistent, which indicated that the method proposed in this work was accurate and reliable.

Kamile Arkin: . Yuxin Zheng: . Yuyang Bei: . Xuesong Ma: . Weilong Che: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Qingkun Shang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22172021, 21872022, 21573039).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.142552.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
